title: Service Dependency Health Propagation
spl:
| inputlookup itsi_services
| search is_enabled=1
| join type=left max=1 service_name [
  search index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0
  | stats latest(health_score) as health by service_name
]
| where health < 50
| table service_name, health, dependent_services
description: Upstream dependency failure should roll up to dependent services; missing links cause wrong prioritization.
value: Upstream dependency failure should roll up to dependent services; missing links cause wrong prioritization.
implementation: Validate service dependencies in ITSI. When a dependency drops below threshold, confirm dependent service health reflects impact (or use entity rules). Run weekly health of dependency graph completeness.
visualization: Service Analyzer tree, Sankey (dependency impact), Table (dependency × health).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI Service Analyzer.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: ITSI service topology, `itsi_summary`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Validate service dependencies in ITSI. When a dependency drops below threshold, confirm dependent service health reflects impact (or use entity rules). Run weekly health of dependency graph completeness.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup itsi_services
| search is_enabled=1
| join type=left max=1 service_name [
  search index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0
  | stats latest(health_score) as health by service_name
]
| where health < 50
| table service_name, health, dependent_services
```

Understanding this SPL

**Service Dependency Health Propagation** — Upstream dependency failure should roll up to dependent services; missing links cause wrong prioritization.

Documented **Data sources**: ITSI service topology, `itsi_summary`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI Service Analyzer. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where health < 50` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Service Dependency Health Propagation**): table service_name, health, dependent_services


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search when you know the service or episode context. Compare with the ITSI Service Analyzer, episode or notable views, and Glass Tables or drill-downs for the same time range.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Service Analyzer tree, Sankey (dependency impact), Table (dependency × health).
grandmaExplanation: We track service health, episodes, and related signals in one workspace so the team can see what broke and who owns it without juggling every console.
knownFalsePositives: Episode and score noise from content changes, seasonality, and who is in scope. We check maintenance and drill paths before we escalate.
